entries.title: Mesopotamian extispicy — reading the liver
entries.blurb: Babylonian bārû-priests read the future in the entrails of sacrificed animals, mapping the liver against the sky. This is the direct ancestor of Etruscan and Roman haruspicy.
entries.mtg: The gut-gazing diviner is the oldest form of the seer MTG paints in blue and white.
entries.source: Jean Bottéro, Mesopotamia: Writing, Reasoning, and the Gods

entries.title: Egyptian heka — magic as a cosmic force
entries.blurb: In Egyptian thought heka was not illicit but a fundamental power of creation, wielded by gods and priests alike. Magic and religion were one system.
entries.mtg: The idea that magic is a neutral, colour-blind energy underlies MTG's whole mana system.
entries.source: Geraldine Pinch, Magic in Ancient Egypt

entries.title: Egyptian amulets and the Book of the Dead
entries.blurb: Funerary papyri specify amulets — the scarab, the ankh, the wedjat eye — to be placed on the body, each a consecrated object of protection for the journey through death.
entries.mtg: The oldest wearable technology; the ancestor of every protective artifact.
entries.source: Geraldine Pinch, Magic in Ancient Egypt

entries.title: Zoroaster and the Persian magi
entries.blurb: The maguš, the Zoroastrian priestly caste of Persia, tended fire and read the stars. Their name would become the Greek magos and the root of magic, magician, and mage.
entries.mtg: Every 'mage' subtype on a card descends, by name, from this Persian priesthood.

entries.title: Homer's nekuia — Odysseus questions the dead
entries.blurb: In Odyssey XI, Odysseus digs a pit, fills it with blood, and summons the shades to speak. This is necromancy in its original sense: divination BY the dead, not the raising of them.
entries.mtg: The reanimator is a modern mutation; the original necromancer merely listened.
entries.source: Homer, Odyssey XI; Daniel Ogden, Greek and Roman Necromancy

entries.title: Etruscan haruspicy passes to Rome
entries.blurb: The Etruscans bequeathed to Rome the disciplina of the haruspex — reading livers against a bronze template of the heavens. The Piacenza Liver survives as its map.

entries.title: The Sibylline tradition
entries.blurb: The Sibyls — freelance prophetesses at Cumae, Delphi, Erythrae — spoke ecstatic oracles. Rome kept Sibylline Books consulted in crisis for a thousand years.
entries.mtg: The prophetess who out-ranked the institutional oracle.
entries.source: H. W. Parke, Sibyls and Sibylline Prophecy

entries.title: Greek curse tablets (katadesmoi / defixiones)
entries.blurb: Thin lead sheets inscribed with binding curses — against rivals, lovers, chariot teams — were folded, pierced, and buried. Thousands survive: the archaeology of the everyday curse.
entries.mtg: The oldest evidence of magic as a hostile, targeted act — an Aura you'd never want.
entries.source: John Gager, Curse Tablets and Binding Spells from the Ancient World

entries.title: ‘mage’ — Persian origin
entries.blurb: Latin magus ← Greek magos ← Old Persian maguš The magi were the Zoroastrian priestly caste of Persia. Their name is the root of magic, magician, and the biblical ‘wise men.’

entries.title: ‘magus’ — Persian origin
entries.blurb: Latin magus ← Greek magos ← Old Persian maguš Same root as mage; ‘magus’ keeps the loftier, hieratic flavour of the Persian priest-astrologer.

entries.title: The goēs — the sorcerer who howls
entries.blurb: Archaic Greek knew the goēs, a wailing ritual specialist who conducted souls and worked charms with cries. His art, goēteia, became the low, coercive magic opposed to philosophy.
entries.source: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. goetia; Sarah Iles Johnston, Restless Dead

entries.title: Herodotus describes the magi
entries.blurb: Herodotus' Histories give Greece its picture of the Persian magi as dream-interpreters and sacrificers — the first ethnography of a magical priesthood.

entries.title: The Eleusinian Mysteries at their height
entries.blurb: Initiates (mystai) at Eleusis swore silence and 'closed their eyes' to the outer world — the root sense of mystic, from myein, 'to shut.' The mysteries promised a blessed afterlife.
entries.mtg: The initiate-only knowledge that gives us 'mystic' and 'mystery.'

entries.title: ‘cleric’ — Greek origin
entries.blurb: Late Latin clericus ← Greek klērikos, from klēros ‘a lot, inheritance’ The clergy are ‘God’s lot’ (klēros) — the same fate/lot metaphor that hides inside ‘sorcerer,’ pointed the opposite, holy direction.

entries.title: ‘necromancer’ — Greek origin
entries.blurb: Greek nekromanteia: nekros (‘corpse’) + manteia (‘divination’) Originally divination BY the dead, not raising them. Medieval Latin folk-etymologised it to nigromantia — ‘black arts’ — swapping death for the colour black.

entries.title: ‘prophesy’ — Greek origin
entries.blurb: Greek prophētēs ‘one who speaks before/for’ (pro- + phēnai ‘to speak’) A prophet speaks FOR a god (and thus, often, before the event). The spelling split prophecy (noun) / prophesy (verb) is a later English tidy-up.

entries.title: ‘sibyl’ — Greek origin
entries.blurb: Latin sibylla ← Greek sibylla ‘prophetess’ The Sibyls were antiquity’s freelance prophetesses; the Cumaean Sibyl guided Aeneas to the underworld.

entries.title: ‘hermetic’ — Greek origin
entries.blurb: From Hermes Trismegistus, legendary sage of the alchemical texts The airtight ‘hermetic seal’ is named for the alchemists’ god of the sealed vessel — hidden knowledge kept literally airtight.

entries.title: ‘grimoire’ — Greek origin
entries.blurb: French grimoire, an alteration of grammaire ‘grammar’ A grimoire is, etymologically, just a ‘grammar’ — a book of learning. When most learning was Latin and mysterious, the grammar-book became the spell-book.

entries.title: ‘glamour’ — Greek origin
entries.blurb: Scots, an alteration of ‘grammar’ (occult learning) Astonishingly, glamour is ALSO a mutation of ‘grammar’ — a second magic-word born from the mystique of book-learning. Fashion-glamour is a 20c. softening of ‘a magic illusion.’

entries.title: ‘tome’ — Greek origin
entries.blurb: Latin tomus ← Greek tomos ‘a slice, a roll of papyrus’, from temnein ‘to cut’ A tome is literally a ‘cut’ — one section sliced from a longer work. The weighty modern sense is a later inflation.

entries.title: ‘phylactery’ — Greek origin
entries.blurb: Greek phylaktērion ‘a safeguard, amulet’, from phylassein ‘to guard’ First the Jewish tefillin, then any protective amulet or scripture-case. Modern fantasy (D&D) reassigned it to a lich’s soul-jar — a total shift of referent while keeping the ‘container of power’ core.

entries.title: ‘pentacle’ — Greek origin
entries.blurb: Medieval Latin pentaculum, ult. Greek pente ‘five’ A five-figured talismanic disc (the ‘pentagram’ is strictly the drawn star; the pentacle is the whole charged object). Renaissance magicians wore them for protection during conjuration.

entries.title: ‘mystic’ — Greek origin
entries.blurb: Greek mystikos, from mystēs ‘an initiate’, from myein ‘to shut (the eyes/lips)’ A mystic is one who has ‘shut their eyes’ — closed to the outer world, sworn to secrecy at the mysteries. Same root as ‘mystery’ and ‘mute.’

entries.title: ‘theurgy’ — Greek origin
entries.blurb: Greek theourgia ‘god-working’ (theos ‘god’ + -ergon ‘work’) The Neoplatonists’ high, sacramental magic — working WITH the gods to ascend — as opposed to goēteia, the low magic of coercion.

entries.title: ‘goetia’ — Greek origin
entries.blurb: Greek goēteia ‘sorcery’, from goēs ‘a sorcerer who howls charms’ The ‘howling’ root marks goetia as ritual noise-magic — the coercive spirit-binding later catalogued in the Lesser Key of Solomon.

entries.title: ‘hierophant’ — Greek origin
entries.blurb: Greek hierophantēs 'revealer of holy things' (hieros 'sacred' + phainein 'to show') The chief priest of the Eleusinian Mysteries, who displayed the sacred objects. Now also a Tarot trump.

entries.title: ‘thaumaturge’ — Greek origin
entries.blurb: Greek thaumatourgos 'wonder-worker' (thauma 'wonder' + ergon 'work') Applied first to miracle-working saints, then to conjurers. Same ergon ('work') that hides in theurgy and energy.

entries.title: ‘exorcist’ — Greek origin
entries.blurb: Greek exorkistēs, from exorkizein 'to bind by oath' (ex- + horkos 'oath') You don't expel a demon by force but by a stronger oath — the same oath-logic as conjure and abjure.

entries.title: ‘exorcism’ — Greek origin
entries.blurb: Greek exorkismos, from exorkizein 'to bind by oath' The rite names its method: not force but a superior oath sworn against the spirit.

entries.title: ‘pyromancy’ — Greek origin
entries.blurb: Greek pyr 'fire' + manteia 'divination' One of dozens the -mancy suffix generates. Fire, being alive and unpredictable, was among the oldest oracles.

entries.title: ‘geomancy’ — Greek origin
entries.blurb: Greek gē 'earth' + manteia 'divination' Divination by dots and earth-figures; it entered Europe from the Arabic 'ilm al-raml, the 'science of sand'.

entries.title: ‘hydromancy’ — Greek origin
entries.blurb: Greek hydōr 'water' + manteia 'divination' Reading the future in water — the bowl of water behind the very idea of scrying.

entries.title: ‘astrology’ — Greek origin
entries.blurb: Greek astron 'star' + -logia 'study' Astrology and astronomy were a single science until the 17th century pried them apart; Kepler cast horoscopes for a living.

entries.title: ‘oneiromancy’ — Greek origin
entries.blurb: Greek oneiros 'dream' + manteia 'divination' Dream-divination, systematised in Artemidorus' 2nd-c. Oneirocritica — antiquity's dream dictionary.

entries.title: ‘prognostic’ — Greek origin
entries.blurb: Greek prognōstikon 'a foreknowing' (pro- + gignōskein 'to know') Straight out of Hippocratic medicine (the Prognostics), it drifted into weather-almanacs and fortune-telling.

entries.title: ‘diabolic’ — Greek origin
entries.blurb: Greek diabolos 'slanderer, accuser' (dia- 'across' + ballein 'to throw') The Devil is, by his own name, 'the one who throws accusations'; 'devil' is just diabolos worn down.

entries.title: ‘esoteric’ — Greek origin
entries.blurb: Greek esōterikos 'inner' (esō 'within') Aristotle's 'inner' teachings, kept for initiates, against the 'exoteric' lectures for the public. The word for the members-only.

entries.title: ‘chthonic’ — Greek origin
entries.blurb: Greek chthonios 'of the earth, underworld' (chthōn 'earth') Greek religion split its gods: the chthonic earth-and-underworld powers (Hades, Demeter) against the bright Olympians above.

entries.title: ‘crystal-ball’ — Greek origin
entries.blurb: Greek krystallos 'ice, rock-crystal' (kryos 'frost') + ball The Greeks believed clear quartz was ice frozen so hard it could never melt — so the scryer gazes into 'a ball of eternal ice'.

entries.title: Aristotle's fifth element (aether)
entries.blurb: Beyond earth, water, air and fire, Aristotle posited a fifth, incorruptible element composing the heavens. Latinised as the quinta essentia, it became the alchemists' goal.
entries.source: Aristotle, De Caelo; Lyndy Abraham, A Dictionary of Alchemical Imagery

entries.title: Theophrastus and the 'characters'
entries.blurb: Theophrastus' Characters sketched moral types; the Greek charaktēr, 'an engraved mark,' would later name the sigil-signs inscribed on talismans to seize a power.

entries.title: ‘druid’ — Celtic origin
entries.blurb: Latin druidēs ← Gaulish ← Proto-Celtic *dru-wid- ‘oak-knower / strong-seer’ *dru- (‘oak’ / ‘strong’) + *weid- (‘to see, to know’). The same *weid- gives us wit, vision, and Sanskrit Veda.

entries.title: ‘geas’ — Celtic origin
entries.blurb: Irish geis ‘a taboo, a solemn injunction’ In Irish myth a geas is a binding prohibition laid on a hero — break it and doom follows. Fantasy borrowed it as a compulsion spell.

entries.title: Berossus brings Babylonian astrology to Greece
entries.blurb: The Babylonian priest Berossus founded a school on Kos, carrying celestial divination into the Greek world, where it fused with philosophy into learned astrology.

entries.title: Bolus of Mendes and sympathetic magic
entries.blurb: Bolus' Physika systematised the doctrine of hidden sympathies and antipathies among things — the theory that lets a talisman work, and proto-alchemy begin.

entries.title: The Greek Magical Papyri (PGM) tradition
entries.blurb: A cache of Greco-Egyptian papyri preserves working spells: recipes for love, dreams, divination, and the compelling of gods. The single richest source for ancient magical practice.
entries.mtg: The actual spellbooks of antiquity — where 'spell' means a formula that does something.
entries.source: Hans Dieter Betz, The Greek Magical Papyri in Translation

entries.title: Cicero's De Divinatione
entries.blurb: Cicero's dialogue weighs — and largely doubts — the arts of divination even as it catalogues them: augury, haruspicy, dreams, oracles. The first great skeptical treatise on foretelling.

entries.title: Roman state augury
entries.blurb: No public act began without the augurs taking the auspices — reading the will of the gods in the flight and feeding of birds. The ritual survives in the word 'inauguration.'
entries.mtg: Divination as statecraft; the bird-omen behind every 'auspicious' beginning.

entries.title: ‘sorcerer’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Old French sorcier ← Vulgar Latin *sortiarius, from Latin sors, sortem (‘lot, fate’) A sorcerer is literally a ‘caster of lots’ — one who manipulates fate. The word hides an ancient divinatory practice inside it.

entries.title: ‘conjure’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin coniurare ‘to swear together’ (con- + iurare ‘to swear’) To conjure a spirit was to bind it by a shared oath — a conspiracy with the supernatural. Same root as ‘jury’ and ‘conspire.’

entries.title: ‘enchant’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Old French enchanter ← Latin incantare ‘to sing a spell upon’ (in- + cantare ‘to sing’) To enchant is literally to sing over someone. The same cantare gives chant, incantation, and cantor.

entries.title: ‘divine’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin divinare ‘to foresee’, from divinus ‘of a god’ To divine is to do what the gods do — to know the future. Same root as ‘deity’ and ‘divinity.’

entries.title: ‘augur’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin augur, a Roman priest who read bird-signs; perhaps from augere ‘to increase, bless’ The augurs read the flight of birds to bless state actions; ‘inauguration’ still carries the ritual.

entries.title: ‘oracle’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin oraculum, from orare ‘to speak, pray’ An oracle is a ‘speaking-place’ or the speech itself — the god’s mouth. Same root as oration and orator.

entries.title: ‘haruspex’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin haruspex ‘gut-gazer’ (haru- ‘entrails’ + -spex ‘observer’) Etruscan-derived Roman diviners who read the liver of sacrificed animals against a bronze map of the sky.

entries.title: ‘quintessence’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Medieval Latin quinta essentia ‘the fifth essence’ Aristotle’s incorruptible fifth element (after earth, water, air, fire); the alchemists sought to distil it out of matter.

entries.title: ‘transmute’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin transmutare (trans- ‘across’ + mutare ‘to change’) The core verb of the whole alchemical project — to move a substance across the boundary of its own nature.

entries.title: ‘amulet’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin amuletum ‘a charm’; perhaps from amoliri ‘to avert, remove’ Where a talisman is actively charged, an amulet passively wards — its very name may mean ‘the thing that carries trouble away.’

entries.title: ‘sigil’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin sigillum ‘little sign/seal’, diminutive of signum ‘sign’ A sigil is a ‘little seal’ — a sign compressed into a mark of power. Same root as ‘signal’ and ‘signature.’

entries.title: ‘codex’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin codex / caudex ‘tree-trunk, block of wood’ Before paper, the codex was a stack of wax-covered wooden tablets bound at one edge — the direct ancestor of the page-turning book, versus the rolled scroll.

entries.title: ‘arcane’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin arcanus ‘secret, shut up’, from arca ‘a chest, box’ The arcane is what’s locked in the box (arca) — the same root as ‘ark.’ MTG made ‘Arcane’ a literal spell-type.

entries.title: ‘occult’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin occultus ‘hidden, concealed’, pp. of occulere ‘to cover over’ Astronomers still say a moon ‘occults’ a star — passes in front and hides it. The occult is simply ‘the hidden,’ before it was ‘the sinister.’

entries.title: ‘cauldron’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Old North French caudron ← Latin caldaria ‘hot bath’, from calidus ‘hot’ Just a big hot pot by origin — the witch’s cauldron is a domestic object made sinister by association.

entries.title: ‘enchanter’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin incantare 'to sing a spell on' (via Old French enchanteur) The agent-noun of enchant; the -ess form (enchantress) feminised it. A singer of spells.

entries.title: ‘conjurer’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin coniurare 'to swear together' One who binds spirits by oath — and, by the 1500s, the street-performer who only seems to. Both senses survive.

entries.title: ‘occultist’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin occultus 'hidden' + -ist A Victorian coinage for a practitioner of the 'occult sciences' — the word arrives exactly when Spiritualism does.

entries.title: ‘summon’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin summonēre 'to remind privately, hint' (sub- + monēre 'to warn') A legal summons and a demonic summoning share a root meaning 'a formal calling-forth'. Same monēre as premonition and monitor.

entries.title: ‘charm’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin carmen 'song, verse, incantation' A charm is literally a song; a 'charming' person is faintly, etymologically, casting one. The same carmen as in Carmina Burana.

entries.title: ‘possess’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin possidēre 'to hold, occupy' (potis 'able' + sedēre 'to sit') Demonic possession is a spirit 'sitting in' the body — the same root that gives us 'property' and 'possession'.

entries.title: ‘chant’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin cantare 'to sing' (frequentative of canere) The sung root under half the vocabulary of magic — enchant, incant, cantor, and charm's cousin all trace to Latin song.

entries.title: ‘commune’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin communicare 'to share' (communis 'common') To commune with spirits is to hold something in common with them; the same root as communion and community.

entries.title: ‘invoke’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin invocare 'to call upon' (in- + vocare 'to call') The exact opposite of evoke: to invoke is to call a power INTO oneself, to evoke is to call it OUT. Ceremonial magic keeps the distinction precise.

entries.title: ‘consecrate’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin consecrare 'to make sacred' (com- + sacer 'sacred') The sacer root runs holy AND cursed: 'sacred', 'sacrifice', but also 'execrate'. The Romans knew the sacred was dangerous.

entries.title: ‘incant’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin incantare 'to sing a spell on' — the learned doublet of enchant 'Incantation' and 'enchantment' are the SAME Latin word: one borrowed early and worn smooth through French, one borrowed late and bookish.

entries.title: ‘evoke’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin evocare 'to call out' (e- + vocare 'to call') The Roman ritual of evocatio literally called an enemy city's gods OUT to defect before a siege — state-sponsored divine poaching.

entries.title: ‘abjure’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin abiurare 'to deny on oath' (ab- + iurare 'to swear') The anti-conjuration: where you conjure by swearing an oath, you abjure by un-swearing one. Magic runs on oaths, made and forsworn.

entries.title: ‘omen’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin omen 'a foreboding sign'; ultimate root obscure 'Abominable' was long (wrongly) parsed as ab-omen, 'away from a good omen' — a folk-etymology so strong it changed the spelling.

entries.title: ‘portent’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin portentum, from portendere 'to foretell' (por- + tendere 'to stretch') A portent 'stretches forward' — the future reaching back to touch the present.

entries.title: ‘diviner’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin divinus 'of a god' + agent -er To divine is to do the gods' own work — know what is hidden. Water-diviners kept the word literal into modern times.

entries.title: ‘augury’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin augurium 'divination by birds' The state art of reading omens in bird-flight; every Roman public act opened with it, and 'inauguration' still does.

entries.title: ‘auspice’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin auspicium 'bird-watching' (avis 'bird' + specere 'to look') To do a thing 'under good auspices' is to recall the Roman bird-diviner (auspex) who checked whether the gods approved.

entries.title: ‘prescience’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin praescientia 'foreknowledge' (prae- 'before' + scire 'to know') Foreknowledge as a kind of 'fore-science' — the same scire that gives us 'science' itself.

entries.title: ‘presage’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin praesagium 'a foreboding' (prae- + sagire 'to perceive keenly') The keen nose for what's coming; the sagire root also yields 'sagacious'.

entries.title: ‘premonition’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin praemonitio 'a forewarning' (prae- + monēre 'to warn') The warn-root monēre also builds summon, monitor, admonish — magic and administration share their vocabulary of warning.

entries.title: ‘potion’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin potio 'a drink' (potare 'to drink') 'Poison' is the very same Latin word arriving by a different French road — a potion and a poison are perfect etymological twins.

entries.title: ‘crucible’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Medieval Latin crucibulum 'melting-pot, night-lamp' An ordeal is a 'crucible' by metaphor from the alchemist's fierce little pot; medieval scribes linked it to crux, 'cross'.

entries.title: ‘distill’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin destillare 'to drip down' (de- + stillare 'to drip', stilla 'a drop') The whole apparatus is named for dripping; 'still' (the device) is just a clipping of 'distill'.

entries.title: ‘putrefy’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin putrefacere 'to make rotten' (puter 'rotten' + facere 'to make') The alchemical nigredo, the black rotting stage that had to precede perfection — rot as a rung on the ladder up.

entries.title: ‘retort’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin retortus 'twisted back' (re- + torquere 'to twist') The vessel is named for its bent-back neck; the 'sharp reply' retort is the same word (an answer twisted back at you).

entries.title: ‘sublimate’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin sublimare 'to raise up' (sublimis 'lofty') Solid straight to vapour, skipping liquid — a rising. Freud borrowed the word for desire redirected upward.

entries.title: ‘tincture’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin tinctura 'a dyeing' (tingere 'to dye, moisten') A tincture colours a thing AND imparts its virtue; heraldry's 'tinctures' (the colours of a shield) are the same word.

entries.title: ‘vitriol’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Medieval Latin vitriolum 'sulphate', from Latin vitrum 'glass' (for its glassy crystals) The alchemists' motto V.I.T.R.I.O.L. spelled a whole instruction; 'vitriolic' speech still carries the acid.

entries.title: ‘infernal’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin infernus 'lower, underground' (inferus 'below') Hell is, etymologically, just 'the lower place'; Dante's Inferno fixed the fire on top of the geography.

entries.title: ‘reliquary’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin reliquiae 'remains' (relinquere 'to leave behind') A jewelled box for holy leftovers; the same root as 'relic' and 'relinquish'.

entries.title: ‘chalice’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin calix 'cup' A doublet of 'calyx', a flower's cup; the Grail is the chalice raised to legend.

entries.title: ‘poppet’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Old French poupette, from Latin puppa 'doll, girl' The witch's effigy is a 'little doll'; 'puppet' is the very same word, and 'poppet' survives as a term of endearment.

entries.title: ‘censer’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Old French encensier, from Latin incensum 'that which is burnt' Same incensum as 'incense' — and as being 'incensed' (inflamed) with anger.

entries.title: ‘fetish’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Portuguese feitiço 'charm', from Latin facticius 'made by art' (facere 'to make') Portuguese sailors coined it for West African power-objects; anthropology, Marx, and Freud each borrowed it in turn.

entries.title: ‘testament’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin testamentum 'a will', from testis 'a witness' The Old and New 'Testaments' are 'witnessed covenants'. Same testis as testify — and, by an old Roman oath-custom, testicle.

entries.title: ‘folio’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin folium 'a leaf' A book measured in leaves; 'foliage', 'portfolio' (leaf-carrier) and Shakespeare's First Folio all grow from it.

entries.title: ‘bestiary’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin bestia 'beast' + -arium Books where every animal is a moral lesson — the pelican as Christ, the beaver as chastity. Zoology as sermon.

entries.title: ‘manuscript’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin manu scriptus 'written by hand' (manus 'hand' + scribere 'to write') Defined entirely by hand-writing, in pointed contrast to print; abbreviated 'MS', plural 'MSS'.

entries.title: ‘herbal’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Latin herba 'grass, herb' + -al A book of plants and their virtues — where botany, medicine, and magic were bound in one volume.

entries.title: ‘treatise’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: Old French traitis, from Latin tractare 'to handle' (trahere 'to drag') A 'handling' of a subject; the same tractare as 'treat', 'tract', and 'traction' — an argument dragged out in full.

entries.title: ‘book-of-hours’ — Latin origin
entries.blurb: English rendering of Latin horae 'hours' (ult. Greek hōra 'season') The runaway bestseller of the Middle Ages: prayers timed to the canonical hours, and the canvas for its greatest illumination.

entries.title: Simon Magus and the Christian polemic against magic
entries.blurb: The Acts of the Apostles casts Simon 'the magus' as a false wonder-worker, fixing the magus as a Christian byword for illicit power — 'simony' is named for him.
entries.source: Acts 8; Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages

entries.title: Pliny's Natural History on amulets and magic
entries.blurb: Pliny's encyclopaedia records — while sneering at — hundreds of magical remedies, amulets, and the 'vanity of the magi,' preserving a vast trove of popular practice.

entries.title: The Corpus Hermeticum
entries.blurb: Greek philosophical dialogues attributed to Hermes Trismegistus fuse Platonism, Egyptian religion and cosmic piety. Their rediscovery would ignite the Renaissance revival of magic.
entries.source: Brian Copenhaver, Hermetica; RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. hermetica

entries.title: Ptolemy's Tetrabiblos
entries.blurb: Ptolemy gave astrology its authoritative technical form — houses, aspects, dignities — the framework still used to elect the hour for a talisman.
entries.source: Ptolemy, Tetrabiblos; RenaissanceMagicDB, s.vv. aspectus, dignitas

entries.title: Artemidorus' Oneirocritica
entries.blurb: Artemidorus compiled the ancient world's great dream-book, a systematic key to oneiromancy that Freud himself would cite.

entries.title: Plotinus and the Enneads
entries.blurb: Plotinus founded Neoplatonism, mapping reality as emanation from the One through Nous and Soul — the metaphysics that would underwrite theurgy and Renaissance magic alike.
entries.source: Plotinus, Enneads; RenaissanceMagicDB, s.vv. nous, emanatio

entries.title: Zosimos of Panopolis — the first named alchemist
entries.blurb: Zosimos' visionary treatises, full of dreams of transformation and apparatus, are the earliest substantial alchemical writings under a known author — Greco-Egyptian khēmeia.
entries.mtg: The origin point of the alchemy the game turns into a mechanic.
entries.source: Lyndy Abraham, A Dictionary of Alchemical Imagery; Principe, The Secrets of Alchemy

entries.title: Iamblichus' De Mysteriis defends theurgy
entries.blurb: Against Porphyry's doubts, Iamblichus argued that ritual 'god-working' (theourgia) could lift the soul to the divine — the philosophical charter of high magic.
entries.mtg: The high-magic/low-magic split the game mostly erased.
entries.source: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. theurgia; Gregory Shaw, Theurgy and the Soul

entries.title: The Testament of Solomon
entries.blurb: This Greek text has Solomon binding and interrogating demons with a magic ring — the seed of the whole Solomonic tradition that flowers in the medieval grimoires.
entries.mtg: The template for binding named demons — the Ars Goetia's ancestor.
entries.source: Joseph Peterson (ed.), The Testament of Solomon; RenaissanceMagicDB context

entries.title: Augustine condemns divination and omens
entries.blurb: In De Doctrina Christiana, Augustine folds augury, astrology and omen-reading into idolatry and demonic pact — the frame that would criminalise magic for a millennium.
entries.source: Augustine, De Doctrina Christiana; Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages

entries.title: Proclus systematises theurgy
entries.blurb: The last great pagan Neoplatonist, Proclus, wove theurgic ritual into a total metaphysical system, transmitting it to Byzantium and, eventually, to Ficino.

entries.title: Justinian closes the Academy of Athens
entries.blurb: The last Neoplatonist teachers scattered, some to Persia, carrying theurgic Neoplatonism out of the classical world and into the underground streams that would resurface in Islam and the Renaissance.
entries.source: Gregory Shaw, Theurgy and the Soul

entries.title: Isidore of Seville taxonomises magic
entries.blurb: Book VIII of Isidore's Etymologiae lists the kinds of magician — magi, necromantii, augurs, haruspices — a catalogue medieval writers quarried for a thousand years.
entries.mtg: An early attempt to sort magic into types, as our lexicon does.
entries.source: Isidore, Etymologiae VIII; Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages

entries.title: Anglo-Saxon galdor and the metrical charms
entries.blurb: Old English healing charms — the Nine Herbs Charm, Wið Færstice — mix Christian prayer, Germanic galdor (incantation), and herb-lore. Here the English 'spell' (a saying) is still doing magic aloud.
entries.mtg: The homegrown English root of 'spell,' before it meant a card you cast.

entries.title: Jābir ibn Ḥayyān (Geber) and Arabic alchemy
entries.blurb: The vast Jabirian corpus gave alchemy its mercury–sulphur theory of metals and its dream of the elixir, and coined much of its technical Arabic — al-iksīr, al-kīmiyāʾ — that Latin Europe would inherit whole.
entries.mtg: The Arabic engine room of the whole alchemy family of words.
entries.source: Principe, The Secrets of Alchemy; William Newman on the Latin Geber

entries.title: al-Kindī's De Radiis — a physics of magic
entries.blurb: Al-Kindī theorised that everything radiates 'rays' that act at a distance; words, figures, and characters could focus them. It gave talismans and sigils a natural-philosophical mechanism.
entries.source: al-Kindī, De Radiis; Vittoria Perrone Compagni (renaissance magic corpus)

entries.title: Abū Maʿshar, prince of astrologers
entries.blurb: Abū Maʿshar's great astrological syntheses, later translated into Latin, carried Aristotelian celestial causation and the technical apparatus of the horoscope into Christian Europe.
entries.source: Paola Zambelli, Astrology and Magic (renaissance magic corpus)

entries.title: ‘alchemy’ — Arabic origin
entries.blurb: Medieval Latin alkimia ← Arabic al-kīmiyāʾ (al- ‘the’ + kīmiyā) The Arabic preserves a Greek word — perhaps khēmia (‘the Egyptian art,’ from Khem, ‘the black land’) or khymeia (‘pouring, infusion’). The al- is the Arabic article, as in algebra, alkali, alcohol.

entries.title: ‘elixir’ — Arabic origin
entries.blurb: Medieval Latin ← Arabic al-iksīr ‘the philosopher’s stone’ ← Greek xērion ‘drying powder’ Began as a dry medicinal powder for wounds, became the transmuting ‘stone,’ then the draught of eternal life.

entries.title: ‘azoth’ — Arabic origin
entries.blurb: Arabic al-zāʾūq ‘the mercury’, cross-bred with a mystical alphabet-word Said to be built from the first and last letters of the Latin, Greek and Hebrew alphabets (A + Z/Ω/Tav) — ‘the beginning and the end’ — while also meaning quicksilver. A word designed to be a symbol.

entries.title: ‘talisman’ — Arabic origin
entries.blurb: Arabic ṭilsam ← Byzantine Greek telesma ‘consecration’, from telein ‘to complete/consecrate’ A talisman is a ‘completed’ or consecrated object — made powerful by ritual, not found powerful. Contrast the amulet, which merely protects.

entries.title: ‘amalgam’ — Arabic origin
entries.blurb: Medieval Latin amalgama, prob. from Arabic al-malgham 'emollient, soft mass' A mercury alloy — and another Arabic al- word smuggled into science. 'Amalgamation' of ideas is a 17th-c. metaphor.

entries.title: Old English wicce / wicca attested
entries.blurb: The feminine wicce and masculine wicca appear in Anglo-Saxon texts and law, alongside wiccian, 'to work magic' — one of the oldest and murkiest magic-words in English.

entries.title: ‘rune’ enters English
entries.blurb: An alphabet whose name means ‘secret.’

entries.title: ‘ward’ enters English
entries.blurb: 'Guard' and 'ward' are the same word, split by the Channel.

entries.title: The Ghāyat al-Ḥakīm (Picatrix) compiled in al-Andalus
entries.blurb: This Arabic compendium of astral magic — how to trap planetary spiritus in talismans by choosing the right hour and matter — is the single most important source for later European image-magic.
entries.mtg: The talisman-maker's handbook; every 'Talisman' card is downstream of it.
entries.source: Attrell & Porreca (trans.), Picatrix; RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. picatrix

entries.title: ‘wizard’ — Old English origin
entries.blurb: Middle English wys (‘wise’) + -ard A wizard was first simply a wise man; the sinister magical sense is a later hardening of an honorific.

entries.title: ‘witch’ — Old English origin
entries.blurb: Old English wicce (fem.) / wicca (masc.), akin to wiccian ‘to practise magic’ One of the oldest magic-words in English, and one of the murkiest — its pre-history is genuinely uncertain, perhaps tied to a root for ‘to bend’ or ‘sacred rite.’

entries.title: ‘warlock’ — Old English origin
entries.blurb: Old English wǣrloga ‘oath-breaker’ (wǣr ‘pledge’ + -loga ‘liar’) Not a male counterpart to ‘witch’ by origin at all — a warlock is an oath-breaker, a covenant-traitor.

entries.title: ‘rune’ — Old English origin
entries.blurb: Old English rūn ‘secret, mystery, whisper’ ← Proto-Germanic *rūnō The runic letters were named for secrecy itself — to know the runes was to know hidden things. The Finnish loan ‘runo’ means ‘poem, song.’

entries.title: ‘besom’ — Old English origin
entries.blurb: Old English besma ‘bundle of twigs, broom’ The witch’s broom is, in the oldest English, simply the ‘besom’ — the everyday twig-broom, later the flying steed of the sabbat.

entries.title: ‘bless’ — Old English origin
entries.blurb: Old English blēdsian 'to consecrate with blood', from blōd 'blood' Astonishingly, to bless once meant to sprinkle with sacrificial blood; Christianity redirected its sense toward Latin benedicere ('speak well of').

entries.title: ‘bless’ enters English
entries.blurb: The bloody pagan root of 'bless'.

entries.title: ‘spell’ — Old English origin
entries.blurb: Old English spell 'saying, story, sermon' (Proto-Germanic *spellam) 'Gospel' is gōd-spell ('good news'); spelling out letters is the same word (a 'reading-out'). The magic sense is the youngest of the three.

entries.title: ‘ward’ — Old English origin
entries.blurb: Old English weardian 'to guard, watch' (Proto-Germanic *wardōn) Guard is the same word borrowed back through French — a Germanic/Romance doublet. Warden, wardrobe, and toward all share it; now an MTG keyword too.

entries.title: ‘bewitch’ — Old English origin
entries.blurb: Old English be- (intensive) + wiccian 'to work magic' To 'witch', intensified. The fashion sense — 'to bewitch a room' — is a softened spell; now also an MTG keyword.

entries.title: ‘seer’ — Old English origin
entries.blurb: Old English sēon 'to see' + -er The plainest word in the whole divination family — just 'one who sees' — standing among a crowd of borrowed Greco-Latin -mancers.

entries.title: ‘soothsayer’ — Old English origin
entries.blurb: Old English sōth 'truth' + secgan 'to say' A soothsayer is a 'truth-sayer'. 'Sooth' has vanished from English except here and in 'forsooth'.

entries.title: ‘eldritch’ — Old English origin
entries.blurb: Prob. Old English el- 'foreign, other' + rīce 'realm' — 'of an otherworldly kingdom' A Scots survival Lovecraft launched into cosmic horror. It means, more or less, 'from the other kingdom'.

entries.title: Old English wǣrloga — the oath-breaker
entries.blurb: Warlock begins not as a male witch but as wǣrloga, 'oath-breaker, traitor' — a covenant-breaker, later 'one in league with the Devil.'

entries.title: Norse runic magic and the rūn
entries.blurb: Old English and Norse rūn means 'secret, mystery.' The runic letters were named for the hidden power that knowing them conferred; the Eddas credit Odin with winning them in agony.
entries.mtg: An alphabet whose very name means 'secret.'

entries.title: ‘witch’ enters English
entries.blurb: The word older than the witch trials.

entries.title: ‘besom’ enters English
entries.blurb: How housework got airborne.

entries.title: ‘wand’ — Old Norse origin
entries.blurb: Old Norse vöndr 'a supple rod, switch' (Proto-Germanic *wanduz, root 'to wind') The magician's wand is, by origin, a whippy twig you could bend. Latin's rival word virga gives us 'verge'.

entries.title: Michael Psellos on demons in Byzantium
entries.blurb: The Byzantine polymath Psellos wrote a taxonomy of demons and preserved Chaldean and Neoplatonic magical lore, a bridge from late-antique theurgy to the medieval world.

entries.title: ‘curse’ enters English
entries.blurb: A curse-word whose own origins are cursed to obscurity.

entries.title: The Sabians of Harran keep the star-cult
entries.blurb: The Sabian community at Harran preserved Hermetic and planetary religion under Islam, transmitting star-worship and talismanic doctrine that fed straight into the Picatrix.
entries.source: Kevin van Bladel, The Arabic Hermes

entries.title: The Toledo translation movement
entries.blurb: In reconquered Spain, translators like Gerard of Cremona rendered Arabic science — astrology, alchemy, the Picatrix's sources — into Latin, flooding Europe with a magical learning it had lost.
entries.mtg: The pipeline that carried the whole vocabulary of magic into Latin, and thence English.
entries.source: Charles Burnett (renaissance magic corpus); Principe, The Secrets of Alchemy

entries.title: Hugh of St Victor classifies the magic arts
entries.blurb: Hugh's Didascalicon and its successors sorted the forbidden arts — mantic, maleficent, illusory — giving scholastic Europe a map of what counted as magic.
entries.source: Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages

entries.title: ‘hex’ — Germanic origin
entries.blurb: German Hexe ‘witch’ ← Old High German hagazussa ‘hedge-rider’ A young loan-word for a very old idea — the hagazussa ‘rode the hedge’ between the tame village and the wild beyond.

entries.title: ‘banish’ — Germanic origin
entries.blurb: Old French banir, from Frankish *bannjan 'to proclaim, summon by ban' Same *bann as 'ban' and the marriage 'banns' — banishment was a public proclamation of expulsion, a shout as much as an act.

entries.title: The great bestiaries
entries.blurb: Illuminated bestiaries like the Aberdeen Bestiary made each animal a moral allegory — the pelican as Christ, the panther's sweet breath as grace. Natural history as sermon.
entries.mtg: When zoology was theology; the ancestor of the fantasy monster manual.

entries.title: ‘sibyl’ enters English
entries.blurb: The women who out-ranked the oracles.

entries.title: ‘summon’ enters English
entries.blurb: A court summons and a demon summons, one word.

entries.title: ‘bewitch’ enters English
entries.blurb: To witch someone, harder.

entries.title: ‘censer’ enters English
entries.blurb: Incense and being incensed: same fire.

entries.title: ‘crystal-ball’ enters English
entries.blurb: The Greeks thought crystal was permanently frozen ice.

entries.title: Michael Scot, court astrologer to Frederick II
entries.blurb: Scot translated Aristotle and Arabic astrology and wrote on the occult sciences at the Sicilian court, becoming a legend — Dante puts him in Hell among the diviners.
entries.source: Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages

entries.title: The Speculum astronomiae draws the line
entries.blurb: Attributed to Albertus Magnus, this work tried to separate licit astronomical images from illicit necromantic ones — the anxious boundary-work at the heart of learned magic.
entries.source: RenaissanceMagicDB context; Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic

entries.title: ‘scry’ — French origin
entries.blurb: Aphetic form of descry ← Old French descrier ‘to cry out, announce’ Scrying literally means ‘catching sight of’ — a shortened ‘descry.’ MTG turned this quiet divination word into one of its signature keywords.

entries.title: ‘scroll’ — French origin
entries.blurb: Middle English scrowle, alteration of scrowe ‘scrap’, influenced by rolle ‘roll’ The scroll’s name records the losing side of a media war — the format the codex replaced, now revived only in the verb we do to phones.

entries.title: ‘grail’ — French origin
entries.blurb: Old French graal ‘cup, dish’ ← Medieval Latin gradalis Chrétien de Troyes’ ‘graal’ was just a serving-dish; only later did it become the Holy Grail. The word’s own sanctity was retro-fitted.

entries.title: ‘haunt’ — French origin
entries.blurb: Old French hanter 'to frequent, resort to' To haunt first meant merely 'to hang around a place'. Ghosts haunt because they keep frequenting; the spooky sense is a late English gift.

entries.title: ‘clairvoyance’ — French origin
entries.blurb: French clair 'clear' + voyance 'seeing' (Latin clarus + vidēre) A young word, coined by Mesmerism and Spiritualism — the newest arrival in an ancient family of seeing-words.

entries.title: The Picatrix enters Latin
entries.blurb: At the court of Alfonso X of Castile the Ghāyat al-Ḥakīm was translated, and as the Picatrix it became the West's master-text of astral talisman-magic for four centuries.

entries.title: Roger Bacon on the elixir and long life
entries.blurb: In the Opus Majus and his alchemical works Bacon argued that a perfected elixir could prolong life and heal the body — alchemy as medicine and eschatology.
entries.mtg: The elixir of life, argued in a Franciscan's Latin.
entries.source: Meagan Allen, Roger Bacon and the Incorruptible Human (alchemy corpus)

entries.title: The Ars Notoria — magic to learn faster
entries.blurb: A ritual of prayers and visual figures promising the divine infusion of knowledge (scientia) — the medieval student's dream of downloading the curriculum by rite.
entries.mtg: A grimoire that promised instant knowledge — pure MTG card-flavour, six centuries early.
entries.source: Skinner & Clark (eds.), Ars Notoria; Fanger, Invoking Angels

entries.title: Aquinas on demons and the pact
entries.blurb: Thomas Aquinas argued that all magic beyond the natural implied a pact with demons — the theological reasoning that turned learned conjuring into heresy.
entries.source: Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages

entries.title: The Sworn Book of Honorius (Liber Iuratus)
entries.blurb: One of the oldest surviving grimoires, its rites for the beatific vision and the binding of spirits pass by oath (iuratus) from master to sworn disciple.
entries.source: Joseph Peterson (ed.), The Sworn Book of Honorius; Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic

entries.title: ‘sorcerer’ enters English
entries.blurb: Fate-casting: the dice buried in ‘sorcery.’

entries.title: ‘conjure’ enters English
entries.blurb: Every conjuration is a contract.

entries.title: ‘prophesy’ enters English
entries.blurb: Prophecy is about speaking, not (only) foreseeing.

entries.title: ‘cauldron’ enters English
entries.blurb: The kitchen pot that became a witch’s icon.

entries.title: ‘grail’ enters English
entries.blurb: The Holy Grail started as a dinner plate.

entries.title: ‘charm’ enters English
entries.blurb: Charm is a song that binds you.

entries.title: ‘haunt’ enters English
entries.blurb: Ghosts don't haunt — they just won't leave.

entries.title: ‘commune’ enters English
entries.blurb: A séance is a kind of sharing.

entries.title: ‘seer’ enters English
entries.blurb: The homegrown English prophet among the Greek imports.

entries.title: ‘potion’ enters English
entries.blurb: Potion and poison: one Latin word, two fates.

entries.title: ‘wand’ enters English
entries.blurb: The magic wand was just a bendy stick.

entries.title: ‘chalice’ enters English
entries.blurb: Cup, calyx, chalice, Grail — one shape, four words.

entries.title: ‘testament’ enters English
entries.blurb: Why the Bible is named after a legal will.

entries.title: ‘treatise’ enters English
entries.blurb: To 'treat' a topic is to drag it out.

entries.title: Pope John XXII moves against necromancy
entries.blurb: Fearing magical attempts on his life, John XXII authorised inquisition into 'those who make a pact with hell,' hardening the Church's war on ritual magic.
entries.source: Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages; Bailey, The Sacred and the Sinister

entries.title: John of Morigny rewrites forbidden magic as devotion
entries.blurb: A French monk, burned once by the Ars Notoria, wrote his own Liber Visionum — reframing knowledge-magic as visions granted by the Virgin. Claire Fanger's central case for how magic disguised itself as piety.

entries.title: John of Rupescissa and the quintessence
entries.blurb: A Franciscan visionary distilled alcohol and called it the quinta essentia — a heavenly incorruptible drawn out of matter to fortify the body against the coming apocalypse.
entries.source: Leah DeVun, Prophecy, Alchemy, and the End of Time (alchemy corpus)

entries.title: The necromancer's manual (Munich Handbook tradition)
entries.blurb: Handbooks of image-magic and demon-conjuring circulated in a clerical underworld — the 'necromancy' that was really the illicit half of learned magic, copied and hidden by educated men.
entries.mtg: The literal spellbooks the game's flavour imagines.
entries.source: Kieckhefer, Forbidden Rites; Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic

entries.title: ‘alchemy’ enters English
entries.blurb: The Arabic ‘al-’ that smuggled a science into Europe.

entries.title: ‘elixir’ enters English
entries.blurb: How wound-powder became the water of immortality.

entries.title: ‘soothsayer’ enters English
entries.blurb: The last hiding place of the word 'sooth'.

entries.title: ‘poppet’ enters English
entries.blurb: The witch's doll and the pet name are one word.

entries.title: ‘divine’ enters English
entries.blurb: Foresight as a divine borrowing.

entries.title: ‘mage’ enters English
entries.blurb: From a Persian priesthood to a fantasy class.

entries.title: ‘enchant’ enters English
entries.blurb: Magic you sing: the song in ‘enchantment.’

entries.title: ‘augur’ enters English
entries.blurb: Why presidents are ‘inaugurated’ — by bird-omen.

entries.title: ‘oracle’ enters English
entries.blurb: The god’s mouthpiece.

entries.title: ‘transmute’ enters English
entries.blurb: The dream-word of alchemy.

entries.title: ‘scroll’ enters English
entries.blurb: We ‘scroll’ our phones with a dead book-format’s name.

entries.title: ‘phylactery’ enters English
entries.blurb: The amulet that fantasy turned into a soul-jar.

entries.title: ‘mystic’ enters English
entries.blurb: Mysticism means ‘eyes shut, lips sealed.’

entries.title: ‘enchanter’ enters English
entries.blurb: The enchanter literally sings.

entries.date: late 14c.; once a minor clerical order
entries.title: ‘exorcist’ enters English
entries.blurb: Casting out by out-swearing.

entries.title: ‘possess’ enters English
entries.blurb: The property law inside demonic possession.

entries.title: ‘chant’ enters English
entries.blurb: The song hiding in 'enchant', 'incant', and 'chant'.

entries.title: ‘banish’ enters English
entries.blurb: Exile as a public shout.

entries.title: ‘consecrate’ enters English
entries.blurb: The sacred root that also means accursed.

entries.title: ‘pyromancy’ enters English
entries.blurb: The '-mancy' machine: a suffix that mass-produces prophets.

entries.title: ‘diviner’ enters English
entries.blurb: Doing the gods' job.

entries.title: ‘augury’ enters English
entries.blurb: Rome's birds ran the government.

entries.title: ‘geomancy’ enters English
entries.blurb: Dots in the sand that grew into a whole art.

entries.title: ‘hydromancy’ enters English
entries.blurb: The oracle in a bowl of water.

entries.title: ‘astrology’ enters English
entries.blurb: When astrology and astronomy filed for divorce.

entries.title: ‘prescience’ enters English
entries.blurb: Prescience is literally fore-science.

entries.title: ‘crucible’ enters English
entries.blurb: Why a hard test is a 'crucible'.

entries.title: ‘distill’ enters English
entries.blurb: Distillation is organised dripping.

entries.title: ‘putrefy’ enters English
entries.blurb: When rotting was a step toward gold.

entries.title: ‘tincture’ enters English
entries.blurb: Colour as the carrier of essence.

entries.title: ‘vitriol’ enters English
entries.blurb: The glassy salt behind 'vitriolic'.

entries.title: ‘infernal’ enters English
entries.blurb: Hell as 'downstairs'.

entries.title: ‘diabolic’ enters English
entries.blurb: The Devil is, literally, the prosecutor.

entries.title: Eymerich's Directorium Inquisitorum
entries.blurb: Nicholas Eymerich's inquisitor's manual argued that invoking demons was inherently heretical, supplying the legal machinery that would later drive the witch trials.
entries.source: Bailey, The Sacred and the Sinister

entries.title: Chaucer's Canon's Yeoman's Tale
entries.blurb: Chaucer's satire of a swindling alchemist is also a precise account of the craft's apparatus and jargon — proof of how deep alchemy had sunk into English culture.

entries.title: ‘conjurer’ enters English
entries.blurb: When 'conjurer' split into sorcerer and street-magician.

entries.title: ‘exorcism’ enters English
entries.blurb: Winning a swearing-contest with a demon.

entries.title: ‘amalgam’ enters English
entries.blurb: One more Arabic 'al-' hiding in plain sight.

entries.title: England bans multiplying gold
entries.blurb: A statute of Henry IV forbade the 'multiplication' of gold and silver by alchemy — a law that, by its very existence, shows how seriously transmutation was taken.

entries.title: ‘quintessence’ enters English
entries.blurb: The fifth element, literally.

entries.title: ‘invoke’ enters English
entries.blurb: Invoke vs evoke: calling in against calling out.

entries.title: ‘abjure’ enters English
entries.blurb: Un-swearing as a magical act.

entries.title: ‘portent’ enters English
entries.blurb: An omen that reaches out of the future.

entries.title: ‘prognostic’ enters English
entries.blurb: From the doctor's bedside to the fortune-teller's almanac.

entries.title: ‘folio’ enters English
entries.blurb: Books once counted in leaves, like trees.

entries.date: c. 1440 as ‘philosopher, sage’; ‘one with magic power’ by the 1550s
entries.title: ‘wizard’ enters English
entries.blurb: How ‘wise guy’ became ‘spellcaster.’

entries.title: The Corpus Hermeticum reaches Florence
entries.blurb: Cosimo de' Medici had Ficino drop his Plato to translate a newly-arrived Greek manuscript of Hermes Trismegistus first — such was the prestige of the 'ancient theology.'
entries.source: Yates, Giordano Bruno and the Hermetic Tradition; RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. corpus hermeticum

entries.title: Ficino and the prisca theologia
entries.blurb: Marsilio Ficino argued for a single ancient wisdom running from Hermes and Zoroaster through Plato to Christ — the prisca theologia that made pagan magic safe to study.
entries.source: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.vv. prisca theologia, philosophia perennis

entries.title: ‘presage’ enters English
entries.blurb: A sharp nose for the future.

entries.title: Pico's 900 Theses and Christian Kabbalah
entries.blurb: Giovanni Pico della Mirandola proposed that magic and Kabbalah, rightly used, prove Christ's divinity — inventing Christian Cabala and the dignity of the magus-philosopher.
entries.source: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.vv. cabala, dignitas hominis; Yates

entries.title: The Malleus Maleficarum
entries.blurb: Kramer's witch-hunting manual fused maleficium (harmful magic), heresy, and a misogynist demonology into a template that helped power two centuries of trials.
entries.mtg: The dark inversion — magic reframed as a capital crime.
entries.source: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. maleficium; Bailey, The Sacred and the Sinister

entries.title: Ficino's De vita makes talisman-magic philosophy
entries.blurb: The third book of De vita coelitus comparanda turned drawing down planetary spiritus into respectable Neoplatonic natural philosophy, giving the astral talisman an intellectual pedigree.
entries.mtg: Every 'Talisman' mana-rock is this idea in miniature — a made object that channels cosmic power.
entries.source: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.vv. spiritus mundi, anima mundi; Copenhaver

entries.title: Reuchlin's De Verbo Mirifico
entries.blurb: Johann Reuchlin brought Hebrew letter-mysticism into Christian scholarship, arguing that the divine Name itself was the 'wonder-working word.'

entries.title: Trithemius' Steganographia
entries.blurb: Abbot Johannes Trithemius wrote a book that looked like angel-magic and was partly cryptography — the ancestor of both the grimoire and the cipher, and a scandal for centuries.

entries.title: Agrippa's De Occulta Philosophia
entries.blurb: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa's three books synthesised natural, celestial, and ceremonial magic into the Renaissance encyclopaedia of the occult — the single most influential magic book ever printed.
entries.mtg: The master-source that fixed the vocabulary of learned magic our lexicon still tracks.
entries.source: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. de occulta philosophia; AgrippaDOP

entries.title: Paracelsus reinvents alchemy as medicine
entries.blurb: Theophrastus von Hohenheim, 'Paracelsus,' turned alchemy from gold-making toward healing — the spagyric art of extracting the virtues of plants and minerals into medicines.

entries.title: Emblem books are born
entries.blurb: Alciato's Emblemata launched the emblem — a motto, a picture, and a verse combining into a compressed symbol — the form later alchemists like Maier would use to encode the Work.
entries.mtg: The card as emblem: image, name, and text making one meaning.

entries.title: Agrippa's magic reaches print
entries.blurb: Printed in full, the De Occulta Philosophia put a complete system of magic — planetary, angelic, cabalistic — into any literate hand, spreading the arcana across Europe.

entries.title: Della Porta's Magia Naturalis
entries.blurb: Giambattista della Porta catalogued 'natural magic' — the marvels achievable by knowing nature's secret sympathies — pushing magic toward what would become experimental science.
entries.source: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. magia naturalis; William Eamon (alchemy corpus)

entries.title: Weyer argues the witches are ill, not guilty
entries.blurb: Johann Weyer's De Praestigiis Daemonum contended that accused witches were deluded melancholics, not diabolists — an early, brave blow against the trials.
entries.source: Stuart Clark, Thinking with Demons (renaissance magic corpus)

entries.title: Dee's Monas Hieroglyphica
entries.blurb: John Dee condensed the whole cosmos into a single glyph, claiming it unlocked alchemy, astronomy, and the Kabbalah at once — the ultimate sigil.

entries.title: ‘spell’ enters English
entries.blurb: Gospel, spelling, and spellcasting are one word.

entries.title: Dee and Kelley's angelic conversations
entries.blurb: Scrying into a crystal, John Dee and Edward Kelley recorded a whole angelic language (Enochian) and built the Sigillum Dei Aemeth, a great circular talisman, from dictated instructions.
entries.mtg: The scrying mirror and the seal — divination and talisman together at magic's Renaissance summit.
entries.source: Deborah Harkness, John Dee's Conversations with Angels (renaissance magic corpus); RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. sigillum dei aemeth

entries.title: Giordano Bruno's art of memory and magic
entries.blurb: Bruno fused Hermetic magic with the classical art of memory, building interior temples of images to grasp the cosmos — and was burned in 1600 for his pains.
entries.source: Yates, Giordano Bruno and the Hermetic Tradition; RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. ars memoriae

entries.title: Scot's Discoverie of Witchcraft
entries.blurb: Reginald Scot debunked witchcraft as fraud and conjuring-tricks — and in doing so printed real conjuring methods, becoming an accidental grimoire.
entries.source: Keith Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic (renaissance magic corpus)

entries.title: Khunrath's Amphitheatrum
entries.blurb: Heinrich Khunrath's engraved 'oratory-laboratory' fused prayer and alchemy in one image — the magus at once on his knees and at the furnace.
entries.source: Peter Forshaw, The Mage's Images (alchemy corpus)

entries.title: ‘incant’ enters English
entries.blurb: Enchant and incant: one word, borrowed twice.

entries.title: The Rosicrucian manifestos
entries.blurb: The anonymous Fama and Confessio announced a hidden brotherhood of Christian alchemist-sages, sparking a Europe-wide furore and a new myth of the invisible college.
entries.source: Tobias Churton, The Golden Builders (alchemy corpus)

entries.title: Maier's Atalanta Fugiens
entries.blurb: Michael Maier's masterpiece pairs fifty alchemical emblems with epigrams and fugues for three voices — an alchemical book you look at, read, and hear at once.
entries.mtg: Ted's own Atalanta projects grow from this book; alchemy as multimedia emblem.
entries.source: Ted's alchemy DBs; Peter Forshaw (alchemy corpus)

entries.title: Kepler, where astrology and astronomy still touch
entries.blurb: Even as he found the laws of planetary motion, Kepler cast horoscopes for a living and sought a 'harmony of the world' — the two star-sciences not yet divorced.

entries.title: Robert Fludd's cosmic engravings
entries.blurb: Fludd's vast illustrated works mapped a Hermetic universe of macrocosm and microcosm, defending magic against the new mechanical philosophy.

entries.title: Ashmole's Theatrum Chemicum Britannicum
entries.blurb: Elias Ashmole gathered England's alchemical poetry into one volume, presenting the art as a venerable native tradition just as its scientific credibility was ebbing.
entries.source: Stanton Linden, The Alchemy Reader (alchemy corpus)

entries.title: Boyle's The Sceptical Chymist
entries.blurb: Robert Boyle attacked the old element-theories and pushed toward a corpuscular chemistry — yet he too believed in transmutation and lobbied to repeal the ban on it.
entries.mtg: The hinge where alchemy begins to become chemistry.

entries.title: Newton's alchemical decades
entries.blurb: Isaac Newton wrote more on alchemy than on physics, hunting the philosophers' mercury and the principles of transmutation in secret for thirty years.
entries.source: Principe, The Secrets of Alchemy; Newman (alchemy corpus)

entries.title: The Lemegeton (Lesser Key of Solomon) takes shape
entries.blurb: Assembled from older material, the Lemegeton's first book, the Ars Goetia, catalogues seventy-two demons with their seals and offices — the master-index of infernal magic.
entries.mtg: The direct ancestor of every 'bind a named demon' card.
entries.source: Joseph Peterson (ed.), The Lesser Key of Solomon

entries.title: The Key of Solomon proliferates
entries.blurb: Manuscript copies of the Clavicula Salomonis spread the Solomonic pentacles — discs of divine names and characters for every planetary operation.
entries.source: Joseph Peterson (ed.), Key of Solomon; RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. pentaculum

entries.title: ‘shaman’ — Siberian origin
entries.blurb: Russian shamán ← Evenki (Tungusic) šamán A late loan into English from Siberian religion; sometimes further linked to Sanskrit śramaṇa (‘ascetic’), though that’s contested.

entries.title: The Salem witch trials
entries.blurb: Twenty people were executed on spectral evidence in colonial Massachusetts — the most famous, and nearly the last, great outbreak of the witch panic.
entries.source: Keith Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic (context)

entries.title: The decline of magic
entries.blurb: Keith Thomas's great thesis: as insurance, medicine, and the mechanical philosophy advanced, educated Europe quietly stopped believing that magic worked — without ever quite disproving it.
entries.mtg: The moment magic became fantasy — the precondition for a game about it.
entries.source: Keith Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic (renaissance magic corpus)

entries.title: ‘thaumaturge’ enters English
entries.blurb: Saints as the original wonder-workers.

entries.title: ‘glamour’ enters English
entries.blurb: Glamour and grimoire: twins from ‘grammar.’

entries.title: Cheap-print grimoires: the Grimorium Verum
entries.blurb: Grimoires like the Grimorium Verum and Grand Grimoire moved into cheap print, feeding a popular market for spells long after the learned tradition had gone respectable.

entries.title: Britain stops prosecuting witchcraft
entries.blurb: The Witchcraft Act of 1736 repealed the death penalty for witchcraft, punishing instead the pretence of magical powers — the law now disbelieved in the crime.
entries.source: Keith Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic

entries.title: Court de Gébelin makes the Tarot 'Egyptian'
entries.blurb: Antoine Court de Gébelin declared the Tarot a lost Egyptian book of wisdom, launching the cards' second life as instruments of divination.
entries.source: Michael Dummett, The Game of Tarot (context)

entries.title: Mesmer and animal magnetism
entries.blurb: Franz Mesmer's 'animal magnetism' produced trance and 'clairvoyant' subjects — a secular occultism that seeded Spiritualism and the word clairvoyance itself.

entries.title: Barrett's The Magus revives ceremonial magic
entries.blurb: Francis Barrett's compendium recycled Agrippa for a new Romantic audience, quietly reopening the door to ceremonial magic that the Enlightenment had shut.
entries.source: Ronald Hutton, The Triumph of the Moon

entries.title: ‘bestiary’ enters English
entries.blurb: When zoology was theology.

entries.title: Walter Scott popularises 'glamour'
entries.blurb: Scott's Letters on Demonology carried the Scots word glamour — a magic spell that deceives the eye — into standard English, whence it drifted toward dazzle and fashion.

entries.title: ‘clairvoyance’ enters English
entries.blurb: The Victorian séance's own coinage.

entries.title: Spiritualism begins with the Fox sisters
entries.blurb: Rapping 'spirit' communications in upstate New York launched Spiritualism, a mass movement of mediums, séances, and a new industry of talking with the dead.
entries.source: Ronald Hutton, The Triumph of the Moon

entries.title: ‘grimoire’ enters English
entries.blurb: Grammar and grimoire are the same word.

entries.title: Éliphas Lévi codifies modern occultism
entries.blurb: Lévi's Dogme et Rituel de la Haute Magie fused Kabbalah, Tarot, and ceremonial magic into the synthesis that every later occult order would inherit — and coined much of its imagery.
entries.source: Hutton, The Triumph of the Moon

entries.title: Blavatsky founds the Theosophical Society
entries.blurb: H. P. Blavatsky's Theosophy wove Western esotericism together with Indian and Tibetan ideas, globalising the occult and giving 'esoteric' its modern currency.
entries.source: TheosophicalAlchemyDB; Hutton, The Triumph of the Moon

entries.title: ‘chthonic’ enters English
entries.blurb: Earth-gods versus sky-gods.

entries.title: The Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn
entries.blurb: The Golden Dawn systematised Western ritual magic into grades and correspondences, training a generation — Yeats, Machen, Crowley — and shaping nearly all magic that followed.
entries.mtg: The immediate ancestor of pop-culture 'ceremonial magic' aesthetics.
entries.source: Hutton, The Triumph of the Moon

entries.title: Mathers translates the Key of Solomon
entries.blurb: S. L. MacGregor Mathers put the Solomonic pentacles and, later, the Goetia into accessible English — the texts that carried demon-and-seal magic into the twentieth century.

entries.title: Frazer's The Golden Bough
entries.blurb: James Frazer's comparative study framed magic as humanity's first, failed science of sympathy and contagion — a framework that shaped a century of thinking about ritual.
entries.source: J. G. Frazer, The Golden Bough

entries.title: Crowley and the birth of 'Magick'
entries.blurb: Aleister Crowley received The Book of the Law and spelled magic 'magick' to mark the real art from stage tricks, becoming the twentieth century's most notorious magus.
entries.source: Hutton, The Triumph of the Moon

entries.title: The Rider–Waite–Smith Tarot
entries.blurb: Pamela Colman Smith's fully-illustrated deck made every card a readable scene, becoming the template for modern Tarot divination and its imagery.
entries.mtg: The illustrated card as an instrument of meaning — a distant cousin of the MTG card.
entries.source: Michael Dummett, The Game of Tarot (context)

entries.title: Murray's witch-cult hypothesis
entries.blurb: Margaret Murray argued the witch trials had persecuted a real pagan religion — a thesis scholars later demolished, but which directly inspired the invention of Wicca.
entries.source: Hutton, The Triumph of the Moon

entries.title: Lovecraft weaponises 'eldritch'
entries.blurb: H. P. Lovecraft took a dusty Scots word for 'otherworldly' and made eldritch the defining adjective of cosmic horror — the uncanny beyond human categories.
entries.mtg: The horror of the Eldrazi and every 'eldritch' card runs through Lovecraft.

entries.title: Jung's Psychology and Alchemy
entries.blurb: C. G. Jung reread alchemy as a projection of the psyche's individuation — nigredo to rubedo as stages of the soul — reviving the imagery for the twentieth century.
entries.source: Jung, Psychology and Alchemy (alchemy corpus); Lyndy Abraham

entries.title: Graves' The White Goddess
entries.blurb: Robert Graves' poetic 'grammar of myth' gave modern paganism its lunar Triple Goddess, shaping the theology of the Wicca soon to be born.
entries.source: Hutton, The Triumph of the Moon

entries.title: Gardner reveals Wicca
entries.blurb: Gerald Gardner's Witchcraft Today announced a surviving witch-religion — actually a new one, stitched from Murray, Crowley, and folklore — launching modern Wicca and its besom, cauldron, and athame.
entries.source: Ronald Hutton, The Triumph of the Moon

entries.title: Tolkien fixes the modern wizard
entries.blurb: The Lord of the Rings gave fantasy its archetypal wizard in Gandalf — staff, robe, and hidden angelic nature — the mould nearly every later game wizard was cast in.
entries.mtg: The template behind MTG's 1,288 Wizard creatures.

entries.title: Yates' Giordano Bruno and the Hermetic Tradition
entries.blurb: Frances Yates argued that Renaissance Hermetic magic was a driver of the Scientific Revolution — the book that made the serious academic study of magic respectable.
entries.mtg: The scholarship this whole project stands on.
entries.source: Yates, Giordano Bruno and the Hermetic Tradition (renaissance magic corpus)

entries.title: Keith Thomas' Religion and the Decline of Magic
entries.blurb: Thomas's landmark social history explained how and why early-modern England stopped believing in magic — the founding work of the field this project draws on.
entries.source: Keith Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic (renaissance magic corpus)

entries.title: Dungeons & Dragons invents the game wizard
entries.blurb: Gygax and Arneson's D&D turned the magical vocabulary into rules — spell slots, the wizard and cleric classes, and the lich whose soul hides in a phylactery, wrenching that word to a new meaning.
entries.mtg: The rulebook grammar of magic that MTG would inherit and refine.

entries.title: Kieckhefer's Magic in the Middle Ages
entries.blurb: Richard Kieckhefer's synthesis made medieval magic teachable — mapping the 'common tradition' and the clerical underworld of necromancy that our histories keep citing.
entries.source: Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages (medieval magic corpus)

entries.title: Magic: The Gathering — Alpha
entries.blurb: Richard Garfield's trading-card game shipped, and with it a corpus that would grow to tens of thousands of cards — the largest published fantasy vocabulary of magic ever assembled, and this study's raw material.
entries.mtg: Year zero of the corpus: 34,629 unique cards and counting.

entries.title: Harry Potter mainstreams the wizard
entries.blurb: Rowling's series put wizards, spells, and a magic school at the centre of global popular culture, cementing the fantasy sense of nearly every word in this lexicon.

entries.title: Owen Davies, Grimoires: A History
entries.blurb: The first full scholarly history of the grimoire traced the spell-book from the ancient world to the internet — the book about the books of magic.

entries.title: ‘conjurer’ in the card set
entries.blurb: The word appears on Magic cards from 2003; it now titles 11 cards and shows in 2 flavor texts.

entries.title: Scry becomes a Magic keyword
entries.blurb: The set Fifth Dawn introduced Scry — look at the top cards of your library and reorder them — bolting a precise new meaning onto a four-century-old word for crystal-gazing.
entries.mtg: A divination word made a mechanic inside living memory; see the Word vs Keyword chart.

entries.title: Theros: Magic goes Greek
entries.blurb: The Theros set drew on Greek myth — oracles, sibylline prophecy, gods and the underworld — and made Scry an evergreen mechanic, folding ancient divination into the game's spine.
entries.mtg: The game reaching back to the very sources this timeline begins with.

entries.title: Eldritch Moon and the Eldrazi
entries.blurb: MTG's Innistrad horror block climaxed with Lovecraftian world-eaters, carrying 'eldritch' — via Lovecraft, via a Scots word for the otherworldly — onto the cards.

entries.title: ‘commune’ in the card set
entries.blurb: The word appears on Magic cards from 2016; it now titles 9 cards and shows in 3 flavor texts.

entries.title: The Routledge History of Medieval Magic
entries.blurb: Sophie Page and Catherine Rider's volume gathered the state of the field — the scholarly apparatus behind many of this site's Word Histories.
entries.source: Page & Rider (eds.), The Routledge History of Medieval Magic (medieval magic corpus)

entries.title: ‘amalgam’ in the card set
entries.blurb: The word appears on Magic cards from 2017; it now titles 8 cards and shows in 1 flavor texts.

entries.title: Ward becomes a keyword
entries.blurb: Zendikar Rising made Ward an evergreen protective ability — the oldest idea of protective magic, the Old English weardian, formalised as a rules keyword.

entries.title: Strixhaven: a whole university of mages
entries.blurb: Strixhaven built a set around five colleges of magic, each a colour-pair, turning the study of magic itself — the Persian magus's distant legacy — into the setting.

entries.title: Alchemy and the Conjure keyword
entries.blurb: MTG Arena's digital-only Alchemy format introduced the keyword action Conjure — create a named card out of nothing — reversing the word's old sense of binding a spirit by oath into pure creation.
entries.mtg: The etymology turned inside out: from contract to creation.

entries.title: Wilds of Eldraine: fairy-tale enchantment
entries.blurb: A storybook set of curses, bargains, and glamour — the folk-magic end of the vocabulary, where enchantment still means what it did in the ballads.

entries.title: The Vocabulary of Magic (this project)
entries.blurb: This study mines the full MTG corpus for the words of magic, traces each to its historical root through the medieval- and renaissance-magic databases, and lays the whole genealogy out — the timeline you are reading.
entries.mtg: Where the game's vocabulary meets its own five-thousand-year history.
